narration.default: Some values in your program never change while it runs. Pi is always 3.14159..., the maximum number of lives in your game might always be 3, the speed limit on a road is always 60. These are CONSTANTS — values that should be set once and never modified. C lets you mark them as constant so that anyone (including future-you) can't accidentally change them. There are two ways to make constants in C: #define at the top of the file (a kind of search-and-replace) and const inside your function (a real variable that's locked). Today you'll see both. Using constants makes your code clearer (the reader sees PI not 3.14 — they immediately know what it means) and safer (if it can't change, it can't accidentally be wrong).

Lines 3 and 4 use #define — pronounced "hash define". This is a special instruction (note the # at the start, like #include). #define does a SEARCH-AND-REPLACE on your code BEFORE the compiler even reads it. Anywhere PI appears below, the compiler swaps in 3.14 first. Same for MAX_LIVES — anywhere it appears, the compiler swaps in 3. The convention in C is to use ALL CAPITAL LETTERS for #define names (like PI, MAX_LIVES, BUFFER_SIZE) so that anyone reading your code instantly recognizes them as constants and not regular variables. #define is great for constants you might use across multiple files (like a PI you share between math files). It's also great for "magic numbers" — instead of writing 3 in 20 places in your code, write MAX_LIVES once and use the name everywhere.

Line 7 uses const int — a different way to make a constant. Read it as: "constant integer named speed, value 60". It looks just like a regular variable declaration but with const stuck on the front. Unlike #define, const is a REAL variable — it has a type (int), takes memory, and you can pass it around. The "const" part just locks it: any attempt to change it later (like writing speed = 80; on the next line) would get refused by the compiler. Try it — add the line speed = 80; below line 7 and watch the compiler complain. const is preferred over #define when the constant only matters inside one function (like a local speed limit). Both ways work; pick whichever fits the situation.

Click Run. The Console shows three lines: PI = 3.14, Lives = 3, Speed = 60. Each printf used its constant the same way it would use a normal variable. The kid (or you in 6 months) reading this code can see PI, MAX_LIVES, and speed at a glance — much clearer than seeing 3.14, 3, and 60 scattered through the code with no explanation. The general rule: if a value is fixed and meaningful, give it a NAME. Constants make code self-documenting. Use #define for shared values across many files, use const for one-function locks.
